attributes.stem: A 72-year-old man undergoes an oesophagectomy for a carcinoma of the distal oesophagus. Prior to this current problem, he had no other health issues, does not smoke and takes no medications. The procedure is uneventful and the patient is making a satisfactory post operative recovery, able to mobilise fully since the first day following the procedure. He has been fully compliant with his venous thrombosis prophylaxis. One week after the operation he complains of left sided chest pain which is constant in nature and slowly getting worse. He has also been persistently coughing when eating and drinking. On examination he appears unwell. His blood pressure is 112/74 mmHg, an irregular pulse of 128/min and a temperature of 38.5ºC. There are reduced breath sounds in the left lower zone and stony dullness to percussion at the left chest base. The abdomen is soft, is not tender and his lower limbs have no apparent abnormalities. His observation for the previous day were blood pressure 130/90 mmHg, pulse 100/min and regular and temperature 37.4C.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of the problem?
attributes.explanation:
Postoperative fever is a very common scenario and the cause can often be reasoned clinically by utilising the time since operation and clinical signs/symptoms. The specific post-operative day on which symptoms develop may indicate the infection source.

A frequently utilised mnemonic for the causes of postoperative fever is the '5 W's', which also implies the timing of postoperative fever:

  • Wind: Atelectasis, Aspiration, Pneumonia

  • Water: UTI, Thrombophlebitis

  • Walking: DVT, PE

  • Wound: Wound infection, Abscess formation, Anastomotic leak

  • Wonder drug: Drug-related fever (diagnosis of exclusion following elimination of all other causes)

It is important to note that this list is non-exhaustive and there are other causes dependent upon the specific patient. When a patient develops a problem in the post operative period, the clinician must always consider how the problem might be directly related to the procedure itself.

As for the patient in the stem, the timing would best fit an anastomotic leak and the new onset of atrial fibrillation would support such a diagnosis. An anastomotic leak occurs when the anastomosis fails and the contents of the body channel leak. Common symptoms accompanying anastomotic leak following oesophagectomy are fever, tachycardia, chest pain and recurrent coughing with swallowing. The dullness at the left base is most likely to be a fluid collection in the pleural space. A contrast oesophagram would assist in diagnosis.

Basal pneumonia would be more likely to occur closer to the time of operation, however hospital acquired pneumonia (HAP) can occur at any time during the patient's stay. This is a likely differential diagnosis to this patient's condition and would require a full workup to exclude it as a cause.

Pneumothorax is unlikely given the patient's findings on clinical examination and the presence of stony dull percussion on the affected side. A chest X-ray would be beneficial in excluding this cause.

Pulmonary embolus would need to be considered given the timing of symptom onset and the presence of chest pain and haemodynamic compromise. Given that the patient has been fully compliant with VTE prophylaxis and there has been no evidence of DVT, this diagnosis may be less likely but would require thorough workup with CTPA or V/Q scan.

Subphrenic abscess is unlikely given the nature of the procedure and the absence of abdominal tenderness.
learningPoints: Anastomotic leak is a serious cause of postoperative fever, will symptom onset typically occurring at 5-7 days post-op.
references: Manghelli JL, Ceppa DP, Greenberg JW, Blitzer D, Hicks A, Rieger KM, Birdas TJ. Management of anastomotic leaks following esophagectomy: when to intervene? J Thorac Dis. 2019 Jan;11(1):131-137. doi: 10.21037/jtd.2018.12.13. PMID: 30863581; PMCID: PMC6384377.
Moon SW, Kim JJ, Cho DG, Park JK. Early detection of complications: anastomotic leakage. J Thorac Dis. 2019 Apr;11(Suppl 5):S805-S811. doi: 10.21037/jtd.2018.11.55. PMID: 31080662; PMCID: PMC6503265.
